summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers complaining about slow compilation on 'fast dual-core' computers may be using Pentium 4 CPUs, which are dramatically slower than newer architectures despite similar marketing claims. Using compilation and Mozilla benchmarks, he demonstrates that even the most expensive Pentium 4 Extreme Edition performs nearly 50% slower than midrange Core 2 Duo or Athlon 64 processors. The post makes an economic argument that given developer salaries, it's irrational for companies to hobble programmers with outdated hardware when a sub-$1000 upgrade could double their productivity.
key_points: Not all dual-core CPUs are equal - Pentium 4 dual-cores perform dramatically worse than Core 2 Duo or Athlon 64 alternatives
The fastest Pentium 4 (965 Extreme Edition at $1000) is nearly 50% slower at compilation than midrange competing CPUs
Real-world applications like compilers and browsers show even worse disparities, with Pentium D being almost twice as slow as Core 2 Duo
The economic argument: developer time is expensive, so cheap hardware upgrades have massive ROI
Tech Report called the Pentium 4 architecture 'lame-duck' - it's fundamentally flawed, not just slower
money_quotes: Not all dual core computers are created equal. Not by a long shot.
The fastest possible Pentium 4 is nearly 50 percent slower at compilation than a midrange Athlon 64 or Core 2 Duo CPU.
Given how much software developers are paid, it makes no economic sense to hobble them with old, slow PCs based on the underperforming Pentium 4 CPU.
I'm not talking about meaningless synthetic performance benchmark numbers; I'm talking about performance in real world apps that software developers use every day.
You could more than double your performance by upgrading to a middle-of-the-road Core 2 Duo CPU.
Isn't the value of your time worth at least that?
key_insight: Developer hardware decisions should be driven by real-world productivity benchmarks and economic analysis of time saved, not by misleading marketing terms like 'dual-core' or high clock speeds.
